name: Dogleg staircase
description: A staircase composed of two parallel flights connected by a half-landing where the direction of travel reverses through a single 180-degree turn. The lower flight rises from the ground floor to the half-landing; the upper flight continues in the opposite direction from the landing to the floor above, so the two flights run side by side in opposite directions and share a common newel wall between them. This is the most common residential stair: it fits a compact rectangular footprint, keeps the rise comfortable by splitting the climb into two shorter runs, and lands the user on the upper floor close to the vertical axis of the lower entry point. Used to connect any two consecutive storeys in houses, townhouses, inns and castle keeps.
